differentialBuckets.label: Sepsis or serious bacterial infection with organ dysfunction, shock physiology, altered mentation, hypoxemia, oliguria, or rapid worsening
differentialBuckets.action: Assess instability immediately, obtain source and severity data, and escalate if hypotension, hypoxemia, altered mental status, poor perfusion, lactate elevation, or new organ dysfunction is present.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Fever becomes a time-sensitive emergency when infection is paired with shock physiology, organ dysfunction, hypoxemia, or rapid deterioration.

source.source_section: screening, early recognition, infection, source control, and organ-support recommendations
source.implementation_notes: Use bedside findings to trigger urgent reassessment; do not use them to delay cultures, antimicrobial treatment, source control, or escalation when sepsis is suspected.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Sepsis or serious bacterial infection with organ dysfunction, shock physiology, altered mentation, hypoxemia, oliguria, or rapid worsening: diagnostic bucket, mimic, exclusion, or competing frame that prevents premature closure for Fever, infection, or sepsis.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Assess instability immediately, obtain source and severity data, and escalate if hypotension, hypoxemia, altered mental status, poor perfusion, lactate elevation, or new organ dysfunction is present.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not applicable to this diagnostic-frame row; use it to structure the differential and avoid premature closure before applying source-backed tests and exam findings.
differentialBuckets.limitations: This diagnostic frame is a safety check against premature closure; it does not confirm or exclude disease without the linked history, exam, tests, and longitudinal context for Fever, infection, or sepsis.

differentialBuckets.label: Respiratory source: pneumonia, viral respiratory infection, aspiration, COPD or asthma exacerbation with infection, or hypoxemic respiratory failure
differentialBuckets.action: Ask respiratory source questions and auscultate lungs; abnormal lung findings, hypoxemia, or respiratory symptoms should trigger chest imaging and respiratory testing per local pathway.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Pneumonia is a common and management-changing fever source; cough, sputum, dyspnea, pleuritic pain, hypoxemia, and focal lung findings change imaging, antimicrobials, isolation, and disposition.

source.source_section: adult community-acquired pneumonia diagnostic and severity evaluation
source.implementation_notes: Pair respiratory symptoms and lung exam with chest imaging when pneumonia or another pulmonary source is plausible.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Respiratory source: pneumonia, viral respiratory infection, aspiration, COPD or asthma exacerbation with infection, or hypoxemic respiratory failure: diagnostic bucket, mimic, exclusion, or competing frame that prevents premature closure for Fever, infection, or sepsis.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Ask respiratory source questions and auscultate lungs; abnormal lung findings, hypoxemia, or respiratory symptoms should trigger chest imaging and respiratory testing per local pathway.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not applicable to this diagnostic-frame row; use it to structure the differential and avoid premature closure before applying source-backed tests and exam findings.
differentialBuckets.limitations: This diagnostic frame is a safety check against premature closure; it does not confirm or exclude disease without the linked history, exam, tests, and longitudinal context for Fever, infection, or sepsis.

differentialBuckets.label: Urinary or flank source: cystitis, pyelonephritis, infected obstruction, prostatitis, catheter-associated infection, or urosepsis
differentialBuckets.action: Ask about dysuria, frequency, flank pain, hematuria, catheter/procedure, pregnancy possibility, and resistant organisms; add CVA/flank and suprapubic assessment when symptoms support it.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Urinary symptoms plus fever can change urinalysis, culture, renal imaging, antimicrobial choice, renal dosing, obstruction concern, and escalation.

source.source_section: fever history and localizing urinary/flank symptoms
source.implementation_notes: Treat urinary source questions as source-localizing history; tie exam to urinary or flank symptoms rather than performing broad GU maneuvers by default.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Urinary or flank source: cystitis, pyelonephritis, infected obstruction, prostatitis, catheter-associated infection, or urosepsis: diagnostic bucket, mimic, exclusion, or competing frame that prevents premature closure for Fever, infection, or sepsis.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Ask about dysuria, frequency, flank pain, hematuria, catheter/procedure, pregnancy possibility, and resistant organisms; add CVA/flank and suprapubic assessment when symptoms support it.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not applicable to this diagnostic-frame row; use it to structure the differential and avoid premature closure before applying source-backed tests and exam findings.
differentialBuckets.limitations: This diagnostic frame is a safety check against premature closure; it does not confirm or exclude disease without the linked history, exam, tests, and longitudinal context for Fever, infection, or sepsis.

differentialBuckets.label: Skin, line, HEENT, CNS, abdominal, joint, travel, medication, or exposure-related fever source
differentialBuckets.action: Use focused source questions and add source-directed exam maneuvers when symptoms, exposures, high-risk host factors, or red flags support them.
differentialBuckets.rationale: A fever workup should actively search for a source without turning into a nonspecific head-to-toe exam; the source history determines which focused maneuvers are management-changing.

source.source_section: evaluation of fever and source-directed history and physical examination
source.implementation_notes: Screen high-risk sources and host factors first, then activate focused add-ons.

differentialBuckets.management_change: Use focused source questions and add source-directed exam maneuvers when symptoms, exposures, high-risk host factors, or red flags support them.

differentialBuckets.limitations: This diagnostic frame is a safety check against premature closure; it does not confirm or exclude disease without the linked history, exam, tests, and longitudinal context for Fever, infection, or sepsis.

redFlags.label: Sepsis or shock physiology
redFlags.action: Escalate urgently for ED, sepsis pathway, monitored setting, or local emergency workflow when hypotension, altered mental status, oliguria, mottling, severe weakness, hypoxemia, or rapid worsening is present.
redFlags.rationale: Sepsis and septic shock are medical emergencies; bedside severity findings should accelerate cultures, lactate and organ dysfunction assessment, antimicrobials, fluids or vasopressors as indicated, and reassessment.

source.source_section: sepsis as a medical emergency and early treatment

redFlags.diagnostic_target: Sepsis or shock physiology: danger pattern, severity marker, or complication that changes urgency for Fever, infection, or sepsis.
redFlags.management_change: Escalate urgently for ED, sepsis pathway, monitored setting, or local emergency workflow when hypotension, altered mental status, oliguria, mottling, severe weakness, hypoxemia, or rapid worsening is present.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not applicable to this red-flag/escalation row; use it to identify urgency and safety risk rather than to quantify diagnostic probability.
redFlags.limitations: Absence of this red flag does not exclude serious disease; interpret with trajectory, baseline risk, vital signs, exam findings, labs, and local escalation thresholds for Fever, infection, or sepsis.

redFlags.label: CNS, airway, purpura, or necrotizing soft-tissue danger pattern
redFlags.action: Escalate urgently when fever is paired with meningismus, severe headache, photophobia, seizure, drooling, stridor, petechiae or purpura, severe skin pain, or rapidly spreading soft-tissue findings.
redFlags.rationale: These patterns can represent meningitis, encephalitis, threatened airway, meningococcemia, or necrotizing soft-tissue infection and require urgent evaluation before source certainty is complete.

source.source_section: fever red flags and serious localizing findings

redFlags.diagnostic_target: CNS, airway, purpura, or necrotizing soft-tissue danger pattern: danger pattern, severity marker, or complication that changes urgency for Fever, infection, or sepsis.
redFlags.management_change: Escalate urgently when fever is paired with meningismus, severe headache, photophobia, seizure, drooling, stridor, petechiae or purpura, severe skin pain, or rapidly spreading soft-tissue findings.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not applicable to this red-flag/escalation row; use it to identify urgency and safety risk rather than to quantify diagnostic probability.
redFlags.limitations: Absence of this red flag does not exclude serious disease; interpret with trajectory, baseline risk, vital signs, exam findings, labs, and local escalation thresholds for Fever, infection, or sepsis.

redFlags.label: High-risk host or healthcare-associated fever
redFlags.action: Lower the threshold for urgent evaluation, cultures, imaging, and empiric therapy when fever occurs with immunocompromise, pregnancy, recent hospitalization or procedure, indwelling line, transplant medications, chemotherapy, high-dose steroids, asplenia, or frailty.
redFlags.rationale: Host risk changes the meaning of otherwise subtle bedside findings and can justify earlier testing, isolation, specialty input, or escalation.

source.source_section: host risk, exposures, hospitalization, procedures, and immunocompromise

redFlags.diagnostic_target: High-risk host or healthcare-associated fever: danger pattern, severity marker, or complication that changes urgency for Fever, infection, or sepsis.
redFlags.management_change: Lower the threshold for urgent evaluation, cultures, imaging, and empiric therapy when fever occurs with immunocompromise, pregnancy, recent hospitalization or procedure, indwelling line, transplant medications, chemotherapy, high-dose steroids, asplenia, or frailty.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not applicable to this red-flag/escalation row; use it to identify urgency and safety risk rather than to quantify diagnostic probability.
redFlags.limitations: Absence of this red flag does not exclude serious disease; interpret with trajectory, baseline risk, vital signs, exam findings, labs, and local escalation thresholds for Fever, infection, or sepsis.

safetyChecks.label: Measure blood pressure
safetyChecks.action: Document current BP and compare with baseline; hypotension or MAP concern changes sepsis escalation and fluid or vasopressor urgency.

source.source_section: hemodynamic assessment and shock physiology

safetyChecks.rationale: Fever, infection, or sepsis: Blood pressure anchors hemodynamic safety and helps identify shock, hypertensive urgency, orthostasis, or medication-risk context before focused diagnosis-specific decisions.
safetyChecks.management_change: Document current BP and compare with baseline; hypotension or MAP concern changes sepsis escalation and fluid or vasopressor urgency.
safetyChecks.limitations: Single readings can be affected by cuff size, positioning, pain, anxiety, arrhythmia, recent activity, and medications; repeat or compare with baseline when results change management.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not applicable to routine bedside safety data; use this item to assess acuity, monitoring needs, and management safety rather than diagnostic probability.
safetyChecks.diagnostic_target: hemodynamic safety, hypertension or hypotension severity, and medication or fluid-safety context

safetyChecks.label: Measure heart rate
safetyChecks.action: Document tachycardia, bradycardia, or rhythm concern; abnormal rate changes acuity, volume assessment, medication review, and sepsis risk framing.

source.source_section: early recognition and physiologic severity

safetyChecks.rationale: Fever, infection, or sepsis: Heart rate anchors physiologic severity and can reveal adrenergic state, hypovolemia, arrhythmia, infection severity, medication effect, or decompensation.
safetyChecks.management_change: Document tachycardia, bradycardia, or rhythm concern; abnormal rate changes acuity, volume assessment, medication review, and sepsis risk framing.
safetyChecks.limitations: Heart rate is nonspecific and can reflect pain, fever, anxiety, medications, arrhythmia, hypovolemia, anemia, or endocrine state; interpret with rhythm, blood pressure, and context.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not applicable to routine bedside safety data; use this item to assess acuity, monitoring needs, and management safety rather than diagnostic probability.
safetyChecks.diagnostic_target: tachycardia, bradycardia, irregular rhythm, or acute physiologic stress

safetyChecks.label: Measure respiratory rate
safetyChecks.action: Document RR directly; tachypnea can signal pneumonia, hypoxemic respiratory failure, metabolic stress, or sepsis physiology.

source.source_section: respiratory support and severity assessment

safetyChecks.rationale: Fever, infection, or sepsis: Respiratory rate and visible breathing effort are early bedside markers of respiratory failure, metabolic stress, sepsis physiology, or endocrine crisis severity.
safetyChecks.management_change: Document RR directly; tachypnea can signal pneumonia, hypoxemic respiratory failure, metabolic stress, or sepsis physiology.
safetyChecks.limitations: Respiratory rate is often undercounted and may be affected by anxiety, pain, fever, compensation for metabolic disease, or sedation; observe directly when it changes acuity.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not applicable to routine bedside safety data; use this item to assess acuity, monitoring needs, and management safety rather than diagnostic probability.
safetyChecks.diagnostic_target: tachypnea, hypoventilation, respiratory compensation, or acute illness severity

safetyChecks.label: Measure oxygen saturation
safetyChecks.action: Document room-air or oxygen-supported SpO2; hypoxemia changes respiratory support, chest imaging urgency, and ED or inpatient escalation.

source.source_section: acute hypoxemic respiratory failure and pulse oximetry

safetyChecks.rationale: Fever, infection, or sepsis: Oxygenation separates stable outpatient-style assessment from possible respiratory failure, pulmonary source, shock physiology, or need for urgent support.
safetyChecks.management_change: Document room-air or oxygen-supported SpO2; hypoxemia changes respiratory support, chest imaging urgency, and ED or inpatient escalation.
safetyChecks.limitations: Pulse oximetry can be affected by poor perfusion, motion, nail products, dyshemoglobinemia, skin pigmentation, and device quality; interpret with work of breathing and clinical context.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not applicable to routine bedside safety data; use this item to assess acuity, monitoring needs, and management safety rather than diagnostic probability.
safetyChecks.diagnostic_target: hypoxemia, oxygen requirement, respiratory support need, or cardiopulmonary acuity

safetyChecks.label: Measure temperature
safetyChecks.action: Confirm fever or hypothermia and document method; hypothermia or very high fever changes acuity and diagnostic framing.

source.source_section: confirming fever and temperature measurement

safetyChecks.rationale: Fever, infection, or sepsis: Temperature confirms fever or hypothermia and helps frame infection, inflammatory illness, endocrine crisis, or treatment urgency.
safetyChecks.management_change: Confirm fever or hypothermia and document method; hypothermia or very high fever changes acuity and diagnostic framing.
safetyChecks.limitations: Temperature depends on measurement site, antipyretics, timing, immune status, and device accuracy; absence of fever does not exclude serious infection or endocrine crisis.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not applicable to routine bedside safety data; use this item to assess acuity, monitoring needs, and management safety rather than diagnostic probability.
safetyChecks.diagnostic_target: fever, hypothermia, inflammatory illness, infection, or endocrine-crisis physiology

safetyChecks.label: Document mental status
safetyChecks.action: Determine baseline versus new confusion, somnolence, agitation, or inability to participate.
safetyChecks.technique: Speak with the patient and, when available, compare with collateral or chart baseline; document alertness, orientation, attention, and whether the patient can safely cooperate.
safetyChecks.when_to_perform: Perform in every fever or possible sepsis workup, especially when the patient appears ill or high risk.
safetyChecks.diagnostic_target: Sepsis severity or CNS/metabolic involvement: new altered mentation, unsafe cooperation, or deviation from baseline.
safetyChecks.management_change: New altered mental status changes sepsis escalation, airway/safety evaluation, delirium/metabolic workup, monitoring level, and disposition.
safetyChecks.limitations: Mental status is affected by baseline cognition, sedatives, intoxication, pain, language barriers, hypoxia, metabolic disease, and neurologic disease; interpret with glucose, oxygenation, vitals, labs, and collateral history.

source.source_section: organ dysfunction and severity assessment

safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available in the local validated source metadata; treat this bedside finding as supportive and interpret it with the cited guideline, diagnostic tests, and patient context.
safetyChecks.rationale: Sepsis severity or CNS/metabolic involvement: new altered mentation, unsafe cooperation, or deviation from baseline. This basic acuity check helps identify severity, airway/safety risk, escalation needs, and safe interpretation of the selected validated workup.

requiredQuestions.label: How high was the fever, how was it measured, when did it start, and what antipyretics, antibiotics, steroids, or immunosuppressants have you taken?
requiredQuestions.text: How high was the fever, how was it measured, when did it start, and what antipyretics, antibiotics, steroids, or immunosuppressants have you taken?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask for every selected fever, infection, or sepsis workup before narrowing the source.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Establishes fever reliability, tempo, pretreatment, medication masking, and host-risk context.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Changes urgency, culture interpretation, antimicrobial timing, medication safety review, and threshold for escalation.

source.source_section: history of fever duration, medications, and clinical context

requiredQuestions.label: What symptoms localize the fever source?
requiredQuestions.text: What symptoms localize the fever source: cough, sputum, dyspnea, pleuritic pain, sore throat, ear or sinus pain, dental pain, dysuria, flank pain, abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea, rash, wound, line concern, headache, neck stiffness, hot joint, back pain, confusion, low urine output, fainting, or rapid worsening?

options.label: Cough, sputum, dyspnea, or pleuritic pain
Sore throat, ear, sinus, or dental symptoms
Low urine output, fainting, or rapid worsening

requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask for every selected fever workup; this question determines which source-directed exam maneuvers and tests are active.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Localizes the most likely infectious source and identifies sepsis severity clues.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Changes chest imaging, urinalysis/culture, wound or line assessment, abdominal imaging, lumbar puncture or CNS escalation, antimicrobial choice, isolation, and disposition.

source.source_section: source-localizing fever history and physical examination

requiredQuestions.label: Any poor intake, dehydration, dizziness, fainting, confusion, low urine output, or rapid worsening with the fever?
requiredQuestions.text: Any poor intake, inability to keep fluids down, dehydration, dizziness, fainting, confusion, unusual sleepiness, low urine output, severe weakness, or rapid worsening with the fever?

options.label: Poor intake or cannot keep fluids down

requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask for every fever, infection, or sepsis workup because severity and hydration symptoms change acuity even before a source is confirmed.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Screens for sepsis physiology, dehydration, hypoperfusion, encephalopathy, and inability to maintain safe oral intake.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive answers change urgency, vital sign reassessment, sepsis labs, cultures, fluids, antimicrobial timing, ED or inpatient escalation, and safety-netting.

source.source_section: sepsis severity, hypoperfusion, altered mentation, and escalation assessment

requiredQuestions.label: Any cough, sputum, shortness of breath, pleuritic pain, wheeze, hypoxia, aspiration risk, or sick respiratory contacts?
requiredQuestions.text: Any cough, sputum, shortness of breath, pleuritic pain, wheeze, hypoxia, aspiration risk, or sick respiratory contacts?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask for fever even when no source is obvious, because pneumonia and respiratory viral illness are common, management-changing sources.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Screens for pneumonia, viral respiratory infection, aspiration, obstructive lung disease, and respiratory failure.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Changes need for lung auscultation emphasis, chest imaging, viral testing, isolation, oxygen strategy, antimicrobials, and disposition.

source.source_section: adult CAP symptoms, signs, diagnostic evaluation, and severity assessment

requiredQuestions.label: Any high-risk host factor or exposure?
requiredQuestions.text: Any immunosuppression, pregnancy possibility, recent hospitalization or procedure, indwelling line or device, travel, outdoor bite exposure, animal exposure, food or water exposure, sick contacts, sexual exposure risk, injection drug use, or new medication?

options.label: Travel, outdoor, tick, or mosquito exposure
Sexual exposure or injection drug use

requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask for undifferentiated fever and for any fever with unclear source, recurrent course, or high acuity.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies host-risk, exposure, healthcare-associated, travel/vector, animal/food/water, STI, injection-related, and drug-fever contexts.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Changes cultures, isolation, empiric coverage, imaging threshold, public health considerations, pregnancy-safe choices, and escalation.

source.source_section: travel, vaccination, exposures, hospitalization, procedures, medications, and immunocompromising conditions

conditionalQuestions.label: Any urinary or flank source features?
conditionalQuestions.text: Any dysuria, frequency, urgency, hematuria, flank pain, catheter or recent urologic procedure, pregnancy possibility, prior resistant urine culture, nausea, vomiting, or reduced urine output?
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when fever has urinary symptoms, flank pain, hematuria, catheter/procedure context, pregnancy possibility, or unclear source with urinary concern.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Distinguishes lower UTI, pyelonephritis, infected obstruction, catheter-associated infection, and renal source risk.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Changes urinalysis, culture, renal imaging, antimicrobial selection, renal dosing, urology escalation, and disposition.

source.source_section: urinary and flank source symptoms in fever evaluation

conditionalQuestions.label: Any abdominal or GI source features?
conditionalQuestions.text: Any abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice, focal tenderness, recent intra-abdominal procedure, or concern for an abdominal infection source?
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when fever has abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice, recent abdominal procedure, or unclear-source concern for intra-abdominal infection.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Localizes abdominal, GI, hepatobiliary, or post-procedure fever sources.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Changes abdominal exam focus, stool testing, liver/pancreatic labs, abdominal imaging, source-control urgency, antimicrobial selection, and disposition.

source.source_section: localizing abdominal and gastrointestinal fever symptoms

conditionalQuestions.label: Any CNS or meningeal danger features?
conditionalQuestions.text: Any severe headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, confusion, seizure, petechiae, purpura, or new neurologic change with the fever?
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when fever has severe headache, neck stiffness, photophobia, confusion, seizure, petechiae/purpura, or CNS infection concern.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies meningitis, encephalitis, meningococcemia, or other CNS danger patterns.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Changes ED escalation, isolation, empiric antimicrobial urgency, neuroimaging decision-making, lumbar puncture planning, and monitored-care threshold.

source.source_section: neurologic and meningeal red flags in fever

conditionalQuestions.label: Any skin, wound, or line-site source features?
conditionalQuestions.text: Any rash, wound, ulcer, drainage, line pain or redness, rapidly spreading skin pain, bite, recent procedure site, or soft-tissue infection concern?
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when fever has rash, wound, indwelling line/device, recent procedure site, bite, soft-tissue pain, or unclear-source concern for skin/line infection.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Localizes cellulitis, abscess, wound infection, line infection, bite-related infection, or necrotizing soft-tissue danger patterns.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Changes skin/source inspection, wound or line cultures, source control, line removal consideration, empiric coverage, imaging, and urgent escalation for rapidly progressive pain.

source.source_section: skin, wound, device, and soft-tissue fever source symptoms

conditionalQuestions.label: Any hot joint, focal bone, or spine source features?
conditionalQuestions.text: Any hot swollen joint, severe focal bone pain, severe back pain, injection drug use, bacteremia risk, new weakness, bowel/bladder symptoms, or inability to move the painful area?

options.label: Injection drug use or bacteremia risk

conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask when fever has hot joint, focal bone pain, severe back pain, injection drug use, bacteremia risk, neurologic symptoms, or musculoskeletal source concern.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Localizes septic arthritis, osteomyelitis, epidural abscess, discitis, or other musculoskeletal infection source.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Changes joint aspiration, blood cultures, spine or bone imaging, antimicrobial timing, orthopedic/spine escalation, and monitored-care threshold.

source.source_section: musculoskeletal fever source symptoms and red flags

requiredExam.label: Observe work of breathing
requiredExam.action: Document tachypnea, labored breathing, accessory muscle use, speech limitation, or apparent respiratory distress.
requiredExam.technique: Observe the patient at rest before auscultation; note respiratory effort, accessory muscle use, ability to speak, posture, and whether oxygen support is being used.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform in every fever workup because pneumonia, respiratory viral illness, shock, and hypoxemic respiratory failure can present with fever.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Respiratory severity: tachypnea, increased work of breathing, speech limitation, or worsening oxygen need.
requiredExam.management_change: Increased work of breathing changes oxygen strategy, chest imaging urgency, respiratory support, ED or ICU escalation, and reassessment frequency.
requiredExam.limitations: Work of breathing may be subtle in older adults, immunocompromised patients, neuromuscular disease, obesity, sedation, or early respiratory failure; pair with respiratory rate and oxygenation.

source.source_section: acute hypoxemic respiratory failure and respiratory support assessment

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available in the local validated source metadata; treat this bedside finding as supportive and interpret it with the cited guideline, diagnostic tests, and patient context.

requiredExam.label: Auscultate posterior lung fields
requiredExam.action: Listen for focal crackles, wheeze, rhonchi, diminished sounds, or asymmetry suggesting pneumonia or another pulmonary source.
requiredExam.technique: Auscultate symmetric posterior lung fields from apices to bases when feasible, comparing side to side; include lateral or anterior fields if positioning limits posterior exam.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform in every fever workup unless clinically unsafe; prioritize when cough, sputum, dyspnea, pleuritic pain, hypoxia, aspiration risk, or no clear source is present.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Respiratory fever source: pneumonia, aspiration, bronchospasm, effusion, edema, or asymmetric ventilation.
requiredExam.management_change: Abnormal lung sounds change chest imaging review, respiratory testing, oxygen/support, antimicrobial framing, bronchodilator treatment, isolation, and escalation.
requiredExam.limitations: Normal auscultation does not exclude pneumonia or early lower respiratory infection; body habitus, poor inspiratory effort, ambient noise, positioning, and prior lung disease can reduce reliability.

source.source_section: respiratory signs and diagnostic evaluation for adult CAP
source.implementation_notes: Stanford Medicine 25 is used for standardized bedside auscultation technique wording.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available in the local validated source metadata; treat this bedside finding as supportive and interpret it with the cited guideline, diagnostic tests, and patient context.

requiredExam.label: Inspect skin for infection source
requiredExam.action: Look for rash, cellulitis, abscess, wound, drainage, ulcer, petechiae, purpura, line-site inflammation, bite, or rapidly spreading soft-tissue findings.
requiredExam.technique: Inspect exposed skin and any symptomatic, device, wound, foot, pressure, or procedure sites; ask permission before moving clothing or dressings and document areas not examined.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform in every fever workup because skin, wounds, line sites, bites, ulcers, and soft-tissue infection are common visible sources.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Skin, line, wound, soft-tissue, or rash source of fever, including high-risk purpura or necrotizing infection clues.
requiredExam.management_change: A skin, wound, or line source changes culture target, source control, antimicrobial coverage, wound care, isolation, and escalation.
requiredExam.limitations: A limited exposed-skin exam can miss sacral, groin, foot, device, wound, or pressure-site sources; document areas deferred and inspect higher-risk sites when history supports it.

source.source_section: skin examination and localizing fever source

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available in the local validated source metadata; treat this bedside finding as supportive and interpret it with the cited guideline, diagnostic tests, and patient context.

requiredExam.label: Inspect oropharynx
requiredExam.action: Look for pharyngeal erythema, exudate, tonsillar asymmetry, oral ulcers, dental source, thrush, mucosal lesions, or dehydration clues.
requiredExam.technique: Use a light when available; inspect lips, oral mucosa, dentition, tongue, tonsils, posterior pharynx, and visible hydration clues while respecting patient tolerance.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform in undifferentiated fever, sore throat, oral or dental symptoms, rash/mucosal concern, dehydration concern, or unclear source.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: HEENT or oral source: pharyngitis, tonsillitis, dental infection, mucosal infection, thrush, dehydration, or airway-adjacent clue.
requiredExam.management_change: Abnormal oropharyngeal findings change testing, isolation, antimicrobial framing, hydration strategy, airway concern, dental or ENT referral, and escalation.
requiredExam.limitations: Oropharyngeal findings are nonspecific and can miss deep neck, sinus, otologic, or dental sources; airway symptoms, trismus, drooling, or muffled voice need escalation beyond routine inspection.

source.source_section: head, neck, oral, and throat source evaluation in fever

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available in the local validated source metadata; treat this bedside finding as supportive and interpret it with the cited guideline, diagnostic tests, and patient context.

requiredExam.label: Palpate radial pulses
requiredExam.action: Assess strength, regularity, equality, and whether pulses feel thready or bounding in the setting of fever.
requiredExam.technique: Palpate both radial pulses at the wrist, comparing strength and regularity; pair with perfusion appearance and blood pressure rather than using pulses alone.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform in fever or possible sepsis to add a rapid bedside perfusion clue, especially when the patient appears ill, weak, dizzy, hypotensive, or dehydrated.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Perfusion or circulatory stress clue: weak, thready, bounding, irregular, or asymmetric pulses in fever.
requiredExam.management_change: Abnormal pulses change urgency of perfusion reassessment, fluids, lactate or shock evaluation, ECG review when irregular, and escalation.
requiredExam.limitations: Pulse quality is subjective and can be affected by vascular disease, arrhythmia, temperature, anxiety, examiner skill, and cuff timing; use with BP, mental status, capillary refill or perfusion exam, and labs.

source.source_section: hemodynamic assessment and clinical perfusion reassessment

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available in the local validated source metadata; treat this bedside finding as supportive and interpret it with the cited guideline, diagnostic tests, and patient context.

conditionalExam.label: Palpate CVA tenderness
conditionalExam.action: Check for costovertebral angle tenderness when urinary, flank, stone, pyelonephritis, or renal-source features are present.
conditionalExam.technique: With consent and patient tolerance, gently palpate or percuss over each costovertebral angle and compare sides; stop if pain is severe.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Add when fever has urinary symptoms, flank pain, hematuria, stone history, catheter/procedure concern, or renal source concern.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Upper urinary tract or renal source: pyelonephritis, renal colic, infected obstruction, or renal-source fever clue.
conditionalExam.management_change: CVA tenderness with fever changes urinalysis/culture priority, antimicrobial framing, imaging threshold, obstruction concern, and escalation.
conditionalExam.limitations: CVA tenderness is neither required nor sufficient for pyelonephritis or obstruction; body habitus, musculoskeletal pain, analgesia, and patient tolerance affect reliability.

source.source_section: urinary and flank source examination in fever

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available in the local validated source metadata; treat this bedside finding as supportive and interpret it with the cited guideline, diagnostic tests, and patient context.

conditionalExam.label: Palpate abdomen
conditionalExam.action: Look for focal tenderness, guarding, rebound-type concern, distension, or hepatobiliary pattern when abdominal or GI symptoms accompany fever.
conditionalExam.technique: Inspect first, then palpate all quadrants gently before deeper palpation, watching the patient's face and comparing tenderness by location; avoid provoking severe pain unnecessarily.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Add when fever has abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice, distension, GI bleeding, peritonitis concern, or unclear abdominal source.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Abdominal fever source: peritonitis, appendicitis, biliary infection, colitis, obstruction, intra-abdominal abscess, or abdominal source-control concern.
conditionalExam.management_change: Abdominal tenderness or guarding changes imaging, surgical or source-control urgency, stool/infectious testing, antimicrobial framing, and escalation.
conditionalExam.limitations: Abdominal exam can be muted in older, immunocompromised, sedated, obese, pregnant, or analgesic-treated patients; normal exam does not exclude serious intra-abdominal infection.

source.source_section: abdominal pain and localizing fever clues

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available in the local validated source metadata; treat this bedside finding as supportive and interpret it with the cited guideline, diagnostic tests, and patient context.

conditionalExam.label: Test neck stiffness
conditionalExam.action: Assess for meningismus when fever has severe headache, photophobia, confusion, seizure, rash, or CNS infection concern.
conditionalExam.technique: If safe and tolerated, ask about neck pain and gently assess passive neck flexion or obvious resistance; do not force movement when trauma, instability, or severe pain is present.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Add when fever has CNS, meningeal, severe headache, photophobia, altered mental status, seizure, or petechial/purpuric rash features.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Possible meningitis, encephalitis, or CNS infection danger pattern.
conditionalExam.management_change: Neck stiffness with fever changes ED escalation, empiric antimicrobial urgency, isolation, blood cultures, neuroimaging decision-making, and lumbar puncture planning.
conditionalExam.limitations: Absence of meningismus does not exclude meningitis, especially in older, immunocompromised, sedated, or early presentations; avoid forced movement if trauma or instability is possible.

source.source_section: neurologic and meningeal red flags in fever

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available in the local validated source metadata; treat this bedside finding as supportive and interpret it with the cited guideline, diagnostic tests, and patient context.

conditionalExam.label: Percuss posterior lung fields
conditionalExam.action: Add percussion when lung sounds or symptoms suggest focal consolidation, effusion, or asymmetric ventilation.
conditionalExam.technique: Percuss symmetric posterior lung fields side to side, comparing resonance and dullness from upper to lower fields as positioning allows.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Add after auscultation when focal decreased sounds, pleuritic pain, dyspnea, effusion concern, or asymmetric pulmonary findings are present.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Pulmonary consolidation, pleural effusion, hyperinflation, or asymmetric ventilation clue.
conditionalExam.management_change: Dullness or asymmetry supports chest imaging review, pneumonia or effusion framing, respiratory support reassessment, and escalation when paired with symptoms and auscultation.
conditionalExam.limitations: Percussion has limited reliability in noisy rooms, obesity, poor positioning, small effusions, or subtle consolidation; use as an add-on rather than a replacement for imaging when pneumonia is suspected.

source.source_section: pulmonary percussion technique

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available in the local validated source metadata; treat this bedside finding as supportive and interpret it with the cited guideline, diagnostic tests, and patient context.

conditionalExam.label: Inspect painful joint
conditionalExam.action: Inspect the symptomatic joint for swelling, erythema, warmth, deformity, and ability to move when fever has hot joint or focal musculoskeletal symptoms.
conditionalExam.technique: Compare the symptomatic joint with the opposite side when possible; document swelling, erythema, warmth by touch if safe, range limitation, and whether pain prevents movement.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Add when fever has hot joint, focal bone pain, severe back pain, injection drug use, bacteremia risk, or musculoskeletal source concern.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Joint, bone, spine, or soft-tissue source such as septic arthritis, osteomyelitis, epidural abscess, or focal musculoskeletal infection concern.
conditionalExam.management_change: A hot swollen joint or severe focal spine/bone finding changes aspiration, imaging, blood cultures, antimicrobial timing, and orthopedic or spine escalation.
conditionalExam.limitations: Inspection alone cannot exclude septic arthritis, osteomyelitis, or epidural abscess; pain medication, immunosuppression, and limited access can blunt findings.

initialTests.label: Sepsis severity labs and reference thresholds
initialTests.action: Consider CBC with differential, CMP or creatinine, glucose when indicated, lactate when sepsis or hypoperfusion is possible, and review prior cultures or recent antimicrobials.
initialTests.rationale: Objective severity data is needed when fever may represent sepsis; lactate >2 mmol/L, lactate >=4 mmol/L, hypotension, or new organ dysfunction changes urgency and monitoring.

source.source_section: screening, lactate, organ dysfunction, and early treatment

initialTests.diagnostic_target: Sepsis severity labs and reference thresholds: objective test, threshold, imaging, or baseline data that confirms diagnosis, identifies complications, or routes care for Fever, infection, or sepsis.
initialTests.management_change: Consider CBC with differential, CMP or creatinine, glucose when indicated, lactate when sepsis or hypoperfusion is possible, and review prior cultures or recent antimicrobials.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not encoded in this workup-support row unless the cited source validates the exact test threshold; use the cited guideline, local assay characteristics, and pretest probability.
initialTests.limitations: Use local assay methods, reference intervals, imaging availability, specimen quality, timing, medications, pregnancy status, and baseline values when interpreting this Fever, infection, or sepsis test or threshold.

initialTests.label: Source-directed infection studies
initialTests.action: Use history and exam to choose blood cultures when serious bacterial infection or sepsis is suspected, chest imaging for respiratory source, urinalysis or urine culture for urinary/flank source, wound or line culture when present, and viral testing when syndrome or exposure supports it.
initialTests.rationale: Source-directed tests prevent a generic fever workup from missing pneumonia, UTI, wound/line infection, viral respiratory illness, abdominal source, or CNS infection.

source.source_section: infection, cultures, diagnostic evaluation, and source control
source.implementation_notes: Obtain cultures before antibiotics when feasible without delaying urgent treatment.

initialTests.diagnostic_target: Source-directed infection studies: objective test, threshold, imaging, or baseline data that confirms diagnosis, identifies complications, or routes care for Fever, infection, or sepsis.
initialTests.management_change: Use history and exam to choose blood cultures when serious bacterial infection or sepsis is suspected, chest imaging for respiratory source, urinalysis or urine culture for urinary/flank source, wound or line culture when present, and viral testing when syndrome or exposure supports it.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not encoded in this workup-support row unless the cited source validates the exact test threshold; use the cited guideline, local assay characteristics, and pretest probability.
initialTests.limitations: Use local assay methods, reference intervals, imaging availability, specimen quality, timing, medications, pregnancy status, and baseline values when interpreting this Fever, infection, or sepsis test or threshold.

initialTests.label: Respiratory source imaging and testing pathway
initialTests.action: When cough, sputum, dyspnea, pleuritic pain, hypoxemia, aspiration risk, abnormal lung sounds, or no alternative source is present, consider chest X-ray or local chest imaging pathway plus viral testing or sputum testing when clinically indicated.
initialTests.rationale: Pneumonia and respiratory viral infection are common fever sources; symptoms, oxygenation, and lung findings determine imaging, testing, isolation, antimicrobials, and disposition.

source.source_section: CAP diagnostic criteria and testing pathway

initialTests.diagnostic_target: Respiratory source imaging and testing pathway: objective test, threshold, imaging, or baseline data that confirms diagnosis, identifies complications, or routes care for Fever, infection, or sepsis.

dispositionRules.label: Escalate unstable fever or suspected sepsis
dispositionRules.action: Escalate to ED, sepsis pathway, monitored setting, or local emergency workflow for hypotension, hypoxemia, altered mental status, poor perfusion, oliguria, lactate elevation, rapidly progressive symptoms, or high-risk host with concerning findings.
dispositionRules.rationale: These findings change fever from outpatient/source evaluation to urgent infection, resuscitation, respiratory support, and source-control workflow.

source.source_section: sepsis emergency management and escalation

dispositionRules.management_change: Escalate to ED, sepsis pathway, monitored setting, or local emergency workflow for hypotension, hypoxemia, altered mental status, poor perfusion, oliguria, lactate elevation, rapidly progressive symptoms, or high-risk host with concerning findings.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not applicable to this management-change row; use it to connect a finding or result to treatment, monitoring, referral, or disposition decisions.

dispositionRules.label: Use outpatient follow-up only when low risk and safety net is clear
dispositionRules.action: For well-appearing low-risk patients with short-duration fever and likely self-limited viral syndrome, document negative red flags, source screen, follow-up timing, and return precautions for dyspnea, confusion, hypotension symptoms, persistent fever, worsening pain, rash, poor intake, or new localizing symptoms.
dispositionRules.rationale: Low-risk fever management still needs explicit safety netting so serious infection, pneumonia, dehydration, and evolving focal sources are not missed.
